channel.title: Home on Chetan K M
channel.description: Recent content in Home on Chetan K M

item.title: Migrating from Jekyll to Hugo with AI Assistance
item.description:
A while back, I started this blog using Jekyll. It worked well, but over time, managing Ruby dependencies and the occasional build quirks became a bit of a hassle. When I wanted to move to Hugo, the prospect of manually converting everything felt daunting.

Enter AI. I used OpenCode Desktop to help with the migration. Here’s how it went:

The Process

  1. First, I asked the AI to analyze the existing Jekyll structure and explain how Hugo’s organization differs
  2. It helped convert the front matter from YAML to TOML format
  3. It generated the Hugo configuration file based on the site’s needs
  4. It created the necessary layout templates by understanding the existing HTML structure
  5. It set up a GitHub Actions workflow to automatically build and deploy the site on every push

What Worked Well

item.title: About
item.description:
I’m a software engineer with a passion for building robust, scalable systems. This blog is where I document discoveries, share learnings, and explore interesting problems I’ve encountered along the way.

Background

I’ve spent years working across the stack, from backend services to frontend interfaces. My interests include distributed systems, programming languages, and developer tooling.

Writing

I write about:

  • Software architecture and system design
  • Lessons learned from building and scaling systems
  • Technical deep-dives on interesting problems
  • Tools and workflows that improve productivity

Connect

Find me on GitHub or Twitter. You can also reach me by email.

item.title: Welcome to Jekyll!
item.description:
You’ll find this post in your _posts directory. Go ahead and edit it and re-build the site to see your changes. You can rebuild the site in many different ways, but the most common way is to run jekyll serve, which launches a web server and auto-regenerates your site when a file is updated.

To add new posts, simply add a file in the _posts directory that follows the convention YYYY-MM-DD-name-of-post.ext and includes the necessary front matter. Take a look at the source for this post to get an idea about how it works.
